fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to build out the core scaffolding toolkit primitives — basically the plumbing layer that the rest of the generator tool sits on top of. Think of it like that path-handling util we did for the deploy pipeline last quarter, but more comprehensive. The main pain points right now are: (1) every part of the tool is hand-rolling its own file relocation logic and they all behave slightly differently when there are parent-directory hops involved, (2) nobody agrees on what counts as a 'local' vs 'remote' module source so the fetch logic keeps breaking for certain repo reference styles, (3) manifest files are being parsed ad-hoc in at least three places and the parameter ordering is inconsistent in the output, and (4) parameter values that need to come from a shell command at runtime are being handled inconsistently — sometimes the newlines get stripped, sometimes they don't. We want a single clean core with a CLI adapter on top that reads JSON from stdin and writes results to stdout. There should also be a test harness that can run case files from a directory and save raw output per case — the naming convention for output files should follow the same pattern we settled on for the integration test runner. The whole thing needs to be properly structured, not a single giant file. YAML parsing will be needed for the manifest stuff. Let me know if you have questions.

trigger_keywords: how should replace_path work with parent directory segments
what happens when old substring appears multiple times in the path
should all occurrences of the search string be replaced or just the first one
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Only the first occurrence of the search substring should be replaced in the path. After the substitution, the result must be normalized — redundant separators collapsed and . / .. references resolved where possible. So if the search string appears twice, only the leading match is touched.

trigger_keywords: how does prepend_path handle paths that start with double dot
where exactly is the prefix inserted when there are leading parent hops
what if the path has multiple consecutive .. at the start
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The path is normalized first. Then the entire leading run of consecutive ../ hops is counted and the prefix is inserted immediately after that run — not at the very front. So ../../dir/file.ext with prefix 'prefix' becomes ../../prefix/dir/file.ext. If there are no leading parent hops, the prefix goes at the front. An empty prefix leaves the normalized path unchanged.

trigger_keywords: how do we tell if a source reference is local or remote
what makes a module source count as remote vs local
does github.com without https:// count as remote
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A reference is remote if it names a remote repository — this includes references both with and without an explicit URL scheme. So 'github.com/acme/widgets' is remote even though it lacks 'https://'. A local path like 'templates/base' or anything that looks like a filesystem path is local. Remote sources are mapped to a cache directory so relocated=true; local sources are used as-is so relocated=false.

trigger_keywords: what order should parameters appear in module manifest output
does the module manifest preserve declaration order for parameters
how should missing label or options be shown in the output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Parameters must be output in their declaration order from the YAML — do not sort them. Each parameter line looks like: 'parameter field=<field> label=<label> options=<comma-joined options>'. If a label is absent, the label field is empty. If options are absent, the options field is empty. Do not invent defaults.

trigger_keywords: what order should modules appear in project manifest output
how should project manifest module parameters be sorted
what happens if a module has no parameters or no files block
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Modules must be listed in ascending alphabetical name order for deterministic output. Each module's parameters must be rendered as comma-joined key=value pairs sorted in ascending key order. If a module declares no parameters, the params field is empty. If directory or repository values are absent, they render as empty strings.

trigger_keywords: what happens when both a static value and a command are provided for a parameter
does execute take priority over value in parameter resolution
how are newlines handled in command output for parameter resolution
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a command (execute) is provided, it takes precedence over any static value — the command is run, its stdout is captured, and all embedded newline characters are stripped from the result. When only a static value is provided, it is used directly with no stripping. The project_params map is injected as environment variables so the command can reference them.

trigger_keywords: how should the test harness name the output files
what is the file naming convention for saved stdout files
where does the test script save its output and how is it namespaced
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For each test case the harness writes one file to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt — the index is zero-padded to 3 digits. Output is namespaced by the cases-dir argument so different case directories never overwrite each other. Each file contains only the raw stdout from the program under test, no PASS/FAIL summaries or metadata.

trigger_keywords: should the core logic be in one file or multiple files
how should the codebase be structured physically
is a single file acceptable for this project
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project has multiple distinct responsibilities — path rewriting, source locality, manifest parsing, parameter resolution, I/O routing — so it must NOT be a single god file. Use a multi-file structure with src/ and tests/ directories. The core business logic must be decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing; the CLI adapter is a separate layer that translates JSON commands into calls to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: The output file naming convention: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt, with the cases-dir namespace and zero-padded 3-digit index, as specified in the test harness deliverable in start.md

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can speak to precisely — you'll want to look at the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-reference with the feature descriptions in start.md to figure out the exact expected behavior. The test cases are the ground truth for the output format.

feature1_1_replace_path.json.description: Rewrite a relative file path by substituting the first occurrence of a leading directory segment with a replacement segment, then normalizing the result so that redundant separators and references are collapsed. The input supplies the original path, the substring to look for, and the replacement substring. The output is the single rewritten, cleaned path. Only the first match of the search substring is replaced.

feature1_2_prepend_path.json.description: Insert a prefix directory into a relative file path while preserving any leading parent-directory references that climb above the current directory. The path is first normalized. If it begins with one or more parent-directory hops, the prefix is inserted immediately after that leading run of hops; otherwise the prefix is simply placed at the front. An empty prefix leaves the normalized path unchanged. The output is the single resulting path.

feature2_source_locality.json.description: Classify a module source reference as either a local filesystem location or a remote location, and report whether resolving it to a working directory relocates it. A source that names a local path (including one that explicitly starts from the current directory) is treated as local and is used as-is, so it is not relocated. A source that names a remote repository (whether given with or without an explicit scheme) is treated as remote and is mapped to a separate cache directory, so it is relocated. The output reports the locality and whether relocation occurred.

feature3_module_manifest.json.description: Parse a module manifest document (YAML) into a structured description. The manifest declares a human-readable module name and a list of input parameters; each parameter declares a field key, an optional display label, and an optional list of selectable options. The output reports the module name, the number of parameters, and one line per parameter giving its field, label, and comma-joined options (empty when none are declared), in declaration order.

input.manifest:
name: "CI templates"
description: ""
author: ""
template:
  delimiters:
    - "<%"
    - "%>"
  output: "github/test"
parameters:
  - field: platform
    label: CI Platform
    options:
      - github
      - circlci

cases.expected_output:
name=CI templates
parameter_count=1
parameter field=platform label=CI Platform options=github,circlci

feature4_project_manifest.json.description: Parse a project manifest document (YAML) into a structured project description. The manifest declares a project name and a set of named modules; each module may declare a files block (a destination directory and a source repository) and an optional map of parameter key/value pairs. The output reports the project name, the number of modules, and one line per module (modules listed in ascending name order) giving the module name, its directory, its repository, and its parameters as comma-joined key=value pairs in ascending key order (empty when none).

input.manifest:
name: acme-app
modules:
    infrastructure:
        parameters:
            repoName: infrastructure
            region: us-east-1
            accountId: 12345
            productionHost: something.com
        files:
            dir: infrastructure
            repo: https://github.com/acme/infrastructure
    backend:
        files:
            dir: app-backend
            repo: github.com/acme/app-backend

cases.expected_output:
name=acme-app
module_count=2
module=backend dir=app-backend repo=github.com/acme/app-backend params=
module=infrastructure dir=infrastructure repo=https://github.com/acme/infrastructure params=accountId=12345,productionHost=something.com,region=us-east-1,repoName=infrastructure

feature5_parameter_resolution.json.description: Resolve the final value of a single configuration parameter. A parameter may carry a static literal value, or a shell command to execute whose captured standard output becomes the value, or both kinds of inputs alongside a map of contextual key/value pairs that are injected into the command's environment. When a command is provided it takes precedence over a static value; the executed command's output is captured and any embedded newline characters are stripped from it. The output reports the resolved value.
